name: Serapis
description: Serapis is a Graeco-Egyptian deity created during the reign of Ptolemy I Soter in Alexandria. He was intended to unify the Greeks and Egyptians in his realm. Serapis combines aspects of Greek and Egyptian gods. He is often depicted as a bearded man, resembling Zeus or Hades, but also incorporates elements of Egyptian deities like Osiris and Apis. He was conceived as a god of the sun, fertility, healing, and the afterlife. His cult quickly spread throughout the Ptolemaic kingdom and beyond, becoming a prominent figure in the Hellenistic world. Temples dedicated to Serapis, known as Serapeums, were established in major cities. Serapis was seen as a benevolent and powerful god, capable of granting blessings and healing to his worshippers. He was also associated with the Nile's inundation, ensuring the fertility of the land. The worship of Serapis persisted for several centuries, even after the rise of Christianity, before gradually declining and being replaced by new religious beliefs. Serapis represented a syncretic blend of cultures and beliefs, reflecting the complex religious landscape of the Hellenistic period. He embodied the Ptolemaic rulers' efforts to forge a unified identity for their diverse population and served as a bridge between the Greek and Egyptian worlds.
subtitle: Graeco-Egyptian God of Healing and Afterlife

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Serapis
keyMyths.description: Ptolemy I Soter, seeking to unify his kingdom, had a dream instructing him to bring a statue from Sinope. This statue was then identified as a representation of a previously unknown deity, Serapis.

keyMyths.title: Serapis and the Nile
keyMyths.description: Serapis was closely associated with the flooding of the Nile, which was essential for the fertility of the land. His worship was believed to ensure a bountiful harvest and prosperity for Egypt.

keyMyths.title: Serapis and the Underworld
keyMyths.description: As a syncretic deity, Serapis adopted aspects of Osiris, becoming a god of the afterlife and judgement. He offered hope for salvation and eternal life to his devotees.

sources.author: Plutarch
sources.description: A detailed account of Egyptian religion and mythology, including the worship of Serapis.
sources.text: A detailed account of Egyptian religion and mythology, including the worship of Serapis.
